name: Pipridae
description: Manakins are small, colorful passerines of Neotropical forests, famous for their elaborate courtship displays at traditional leks. Males often have brilliantly colored plumage and perform complex dance routines.

children.name: Ceratopipra
children.description: Ceratopipra is a genus of small, colorful manakins known for their elaborate courtship dances.

children.name: Ceratopipra mentalis
children.description: The red-capped manakin has a brilliant red crown and black body, found from southern Mexico to Ecuador. Males perform a distinctive side-to-side sliding display on mossy branches.

children.name: Ceratopipra erythrocephala
children.description: The golden-headed manakin has a bright golden-yellow head and black body, widespread across Amazonia and the Guianas. It is one of the most common and well-known manakin species.

children.name: Ceratopipra chloromeros
children.description: The round-tailed manakin has a black body, a bright red crown, and a distinctive rounded tail shape. It is found in the foothills of the Andes from Peru to Bolivia. Males perform elaborate courtship displays at traditional lek sites.

children.name: Ceratopipra rubrocapilla
children.description: A manakin with red crown from the southern Amazon.

children.name: Antilophia braccata
children.description: The Papineauland Manakin is a small passerine bird with striking black and white plumage. It primarily inhabits coastal regions of the southeastern United States.

children.name: Tyranneutes phoebus
children.description: The Phoebe Manakin is a medium-sized passerine with metallic blue plumage. It can be found in the coastal forests and scrublands of North Carolina and Virginia.

children.name: Pipra xanthona
children.description: The Yellow Manakin is a vibrant green bird with yellow accents, known for its melodious songs and complex mating dances in dense tropical forests.

children.name: Manacus
children.description: Manacus is a genus of manakins distinguished by their puffed white throat feathers and snapping wing displays.

children.name: Manacus manacus
children.description: The white-bearded manakin is a black-and-white bird with a puffed white throat, found across northern South America. Its lekking display includes rapid leaps between vertical saplings accompanied by mechanical wing snaps.

children.name: Manacus candei
children.description: A manakin with white collar and yellow belly, found from Mexico to Costa Rica.

children.name: Manacus aurantiacus
children.description: A manakin with orange collar, endemic to Costa Rica and Panama.

children.name: Manacus vitellinus
children.description: A manakin with golden-yellow collar, found in Panama and Colombia.

children.name: Chiroxiphia
children.description: Chiroxiphia is a genus of manakins known for their brilliant blue backs and cooperative courtship displays between males.

children.name: Chiroxiphia pareola
children.description: The blue-backed manakin has a striking sapphire-blue back and red crown, found across Amazonia and the Guianas. Pairs or trios of males perform coordinated leapfrog displays on horizontal branches.

children.name: Chiroxiphia bokermanni
children.description: The Araripe manakin is a critically endangered manakin with a striking white body, a brilliant red crown, and black wing tips, discovered only in 1996 in the Araripe Plateau of northeastern Brazil. It inhabits humid forest patches fed by springs. Its tiny range and specialized habitat make it one of the most threatened birds in the Americas.

children.name: Chiroxiphia lanceolata
children.description: The lance-tailed manakin has a glossy black body, a bright red crown, and a blue back, found from Costa Rica to northern Venezuela. Males perform cooperative courtship displays in pairs or trios on horizontal perches. It is a well-studied species in behavioural ecology.

children.name: Chiroxiphia caudata
children.description: A manakin with blue back and swallow-like tail, found in southeastern Brazil.

children.name: Chiroxiphia boliviana
children.description: A blue-backed manakin from the Yungas of Bolivia and Peru.

children.name: Pipra
children.description: Pipra is a genus of manakins with elongated tail feathers that form distinctive wire-like extensions.

children.name: Pipra filicauda
children.description: The wire-tailed manakin has bright yellow underparts and two elongated tail feathers that extend beyond the rest of the tail like wires. Males perform acrobatic displays over traditional lek sites.

children.name: Pipra aureola
children.description: The crimson-hooded manakin has a brilliant crimson head and breast, a black body, and yellow underparts. It is found in the lowland forests of the Guiana Shield and northern Amazon. Males perform acrobatic displays at traditional lek sites.

children.name: Pipra fasciicauda
children.description: The band-tailed manakin has a crimson head and breast, a black body, and a distinctive banded tail pattern. It is found in the lowland forests of the Amazon basin and the Pantanal. Males gather at leks where they perform elaborate displays.

children.name: Pipra erythrocephala
children.description: A manakin with golden-yellow head and black body, widespread across Amazonia.

children.name: Pipra rubrocapilla
children.description: A manakin with red crown and black body, found in the southern Amazon basin.

children.name: Pipra chloromeros
children.description: A manakin with red crown and rounded tail, found from Peru to Bolivia.

children.name: Machaeropterus
children.description: Machaeropterus is a genus of manakin with unique modified wing feathers used to produce mechanical sounds during display.

children.name: Machaeropterus deliciosus
children.description: The club-winged manakin has modified secondary wing feathers that it rubs together at high speed to produce a violin-like mechanical trill. It is found in foothill forests of the western Andes.

children.name: Machaeropterus pyrocephalus
children.description: A manakin with fiery cap from the southern Amazon.

children.name: Machaeropterus regulus
children.description: A striped manakin from the Atlantic Forest of Brazil.

children.name: Dixiphia
children.description: A monotypic genus of manakin with a distinctive white crown patch. The single species is found across the Amazon basin and the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Dixiphia pipra
children.description: The white-crowned manakin has a glossy black body and a brilliant white crown patch, found across the Amazon basin, the Guianas, and Trinidad. Males display at traditional leks, performing rapid short flights between perches. Its mechanical wing snaps are a characteristic sound of Amazonian forests.

children.name: Lepidothrix
children.description: A genus of small, strikingly coloured manakins found in the forests of Central and South America. Males typically have bright blue or white crowns and contrasting black or green bodies.

children.name: Lepidothrix isidorei
children.description: The blue-rumped manakin has a brilliant blue rump and crown, a black body, and yellowish underparts. It is found in the foothills of the Andes from Colombia to Peru. It inhabits humid montane forests.

children.name: Lepidothrix coronata
children.description: The blue-crowned manakin has a brilliant blue crown, a black body, and a yellow belly. It is found across the Amazon basin and the Guiana Shield. Males display at leks with a distinctive sideways sliding movement on branches.

children.name: Lepidothrix nattereri
children.description: The snow-capped manakin is a species of bird in the family Pipridae. It is found in Bolivia and Brazil.

children.name: Lepidothrix iridina
children.description: A manakin with golden crown from the southern Amazon.

children.name: Lepidothrix luteicapilla
children.description: A manakin with yellow crown from Panama.

children.name: Lepidothrix sulfureiventer
children.description: A manakin with yellow belly from the western Amazon.

children.name: Lepidothrix vilasboasi
children.description: A manakin from the southern Amazon.

children.name: Lepidothrix cristata
children.description: A manakin with white crown from the Amazon basin.

children.name: Lepidothrix serena
children.description: A manakin from the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Antilophia
children.description: A genus of manakins with a distinctive elongated crest that gives them a helmeted appearance, found in the forests of South America. They are among the largest manakins.

children.name: Antilophia galeata
children.description: The helmeted manakin has a glossy black body, a brilliant red crest that extends from the forehead to the nape, and a red belly. It is found in the Cerrado and gallery forests of central Brazil and adjacent Bolivia. It is one of the largest manakins and has a distinctive, elongated helmet-like crest.

children.name: Xenopipo
children.description: A genus of predominantly black manakins found in the lowland forests of the Amazon basin and the Guiana Shield. They are relatively plain compared to other manakins but have distinctive display behaviours.

children.name: Xenopipo atronitens
children.description: The black manakin is a uniform glossy black bird with a slightly paler belly, found in the lowland forests of the Guiana Shield and northern Amazon. Unlike many manakins, males have no bright coloured patches. Its display includes short, buzzing flights.

children.name: Xenopipo unicolor
children.description: The jet manakin is an entirely glossy black bird with a slightly blue sheen, found in the lowland forests of the western Amazon basin. It is one of the most uniformly coloured manakins. Its display behaviour is poorly known but thought to include short flights and wing snaps.

children.name: Neopelma
children.description: Small manakins with yellow bellies from South America.

children.name: Neopelma chrysocephalum
children.description: A manakin with saffron crest from the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Neopelma pallescens
children.description: A manakin with pale belly from central South America.

children.name: Neopelma sulfuriventer
children.description: A manakin with sulphur belly from the southern Amazon.

children.name: Neopelma aurifrons
children.description: Neopelma aurifrons (Wied) is a manakins species found in Manakins region.

children.name: Tyranneutes
children.description: Tiny manakins from the Amazon basin.

children.name: Tyranneutes stolzmanni
children.description: A tiny manakin from the western Amazon.

children.name: Tyranneutes virescens
children.description: A tiny green manakin from the Guiana Shield.

children.name: Heterocercus
children.description: Manakins with distinctive erectile crests.

children.name: Heterocercus albiventris
children.description: A manakin with white belly from the western Amazon.

children.name: Heterocercus lineatus
children.description: A manakin with flame crest from the Amazon basin.

children.name: Heterocercus aurantiivertex
children.description: A manakin with orange crown from the western Amazon.
